questions.question: What is the biggest hidden cost of an active-active regional design?
options: It requires more thought about write ownership, conflict handling, and failback safety.
It can never improve latency for distant users.
It removes the need for traffic management.
It only works for static content.
questions.explanation: Active-active raises correctness and operations complexity, especially for writes.

questions.question: Why is active-passive often attractive for write-heavy systems?
options: It keeps a single clear writer and simplifies correctness during normal operation.
It guarantees zero failover time.
It lets operators skip replication monitoring.
It avoids paying for standby capacity.
questions.explanation: A single active writer simplifies consistency even though failover may be slower.

questions.question: Which question most directly separates active-active from active-passive?
options: Whether multiple regions must accept the same class of writes concurrently.
Whether the service uses HTTP or gRPC.
Whether dashboards use dark mode.
Whether the frontend is mobile-first.
questions.explanation: Concurrent write acceptance is one of the most decisive topology questions.

questions.question: What is the clearest risk of calling a passive region 'warm' without testing it?
options: The region may fail to absorb traffic because caches, replicas, or automation are not actually ready.
The region will always be cheaper than expected.
Users will only notice lower bandwidth cost.
Replication lag becomes impossible by definition.
questions.explanation: Warm readiness has to be demonstrated, not assumed.

questions.question: Why is failback often harder than failover?
options: Because recovered regions may contain stale state and need controlled resynchronization before taking traffic again.
Because DNS cannot change twice in one day.
Because users reject any recovery plan involving automation.
Because stateless services cannot be restarted safely.
questions.explanation: Failback can reintroduce divergence unless it is staged carefully.

questions.question: Which metric best shows whether multi-region readiness is drifting?
options: Replication lag and failover-readiness checks by region.
Only average response body size.
The number of open browser tabs on operators' laptops.
One global CPU metric across the whole company.
questions.explanation: Readiness depends on data freshness and promotion safety, not generic averages.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with reserving spare regional capacity for failover?
options: Higher steady-state cost in exchange for safer outage absorption.
Lower cost and lower resilience at the same time.
The removal of all traffic-steering logic.
Guaranteed elimination of stale reads.
questions.explanation: Failover safety usually requires paying for headroom.

questions.question: What is the strongest interview move when proposing active-active?
options: Explain which data is multi-writer, which is single-writer, and how conflicts are contained.
Say active-active is always more modern and move on.
Avoid discussing operator workflows to save time.
Assume regional failures are too rare to matter.
questions.explanation: The signal comes from constrained write semantics and operational clarity.
